myths.Agent_1:
Long ago, before the sun learned to burn so fiercely, the sky was ruled by a gentle spirit named Aethel. Aethel loved the quiet earth, but mostly, she loved a mortal weaver named Corin. Every night, Aethel would lower her starry veil, and Corin would weave stories into the fabric of the night sky, creating constellations.

Their love was forbidden by the Elder Winds, who believed the heavens and the earth must remain eternally separate. In their fierce jealousy, the Winds summoned a terrible storm, tearing Corin's loom apart and scattering his threads across the mortal realm. Heartbroken and alone, Corin endlessly wandered the earth searching for his lost threads, unable to look up at the sky.

Aethel, watching from above, was strictly forbidden to descend. Unable to hold her beloved, she wept. Night after night, her silver tears fell gently through the cold night air, landing on the grass, the leaves, and the scattered threads of Corin’s loom.

When the golden sun finally rose, it caught the light of her tears, making the world sparkle with her sorrow. To this day, the morning dew remains—Aethel’s quiet weeping for a lost love.
myths.Agent_2:
Long before humans walked the earth, the mountains were guarded by Aella, a spirit woven from mist and morning light. Aella possessed a voice of unparalleled beauty. When she sang, the pines wept golden sap, and the rivers paused their rushing to listen.

High above, the wind god Boreas grew consumed by envy. His own voice was a harsh, toneless howl that caused creatures to flee in terror. He commanded Aella to teach him the secret of melody, but she refused, knowing his chaotic nature would shatter the delicate harmony of the world.

Furious, Boreas descended upon the peaks in a terrible storm. He trapped Aella within the deep, twisting caverns, using his gales to strip away her physical form until nothing remained but her breath. He believed he had silenced her forever.

But beauty cannot be so easily destroyed. Though Aella lacked a body, her voice lingered in the stone. Unable to initiate a song of her own, she waits in the deep ravines and hollow caves. When travelers call out, she catches their words and sings them back, her lonely spirit reaching out through the ages. Thus, the echo was born, a tragic reminder of the mountain’s first singer.

Myth: Aella was a spirit of the morning mist, her voice a melody that coaxed the sun over the mountain peaks. She loved the world below, but she was bound to the high crags, watched over by the jealous mountain king, Oros. Oros possessed a heart of granite and despised Aella’s gentle songs, for they softened the jagged stones of his domain.

One dawn, Aella sang a song so beautiful that the valleys below bloomed in brilliant colors. Furious that her song reached beyond his peaks, Oros struck the earth, shattering the crags and trapping Aella deep within a lightless cavern. He sealed the entrance with heavy boulders, declaring that her voice would never again greet the morning.

Yet Aella’s spirit was made of moisture and light, impossible to crush. In her dark prison, she wept, her breath turning to a dense, white vapor. Driven by her longing for the dawn, this vapor seeped through the tiny fissures of the rock.

Now, every morning, Aella’s silent breath rises from the valleys and clings to the mountainsides. We call it the morning fog—a trapped spirit forever reaching upward, trying to embrace the sun she can no longer sing to.

Because the wood had been touched by Aethel’s starlight and Corin’s devoted hands, it did not rot. Instead, it took root. From these splintered remnants grew a new kind of tree, tall and slender, reaching desperately back toward the night sky.

When the wind blows through their branches, the trees do not snap; they bend flexibly, a quiet defiance against the very gales that tried to destroy them. The birch remains a living monument to the mortal weaver’s lost loom, forever holding the memory of starlight in its pale, silvery skin.
